article.title: New statue in London, attributed to Banksy, of a suited man, blinded by a flag

summary: A new statue in central London, attributed to Banksy, depicts a suited man blinded by a flag and stepping off a ledge, sparking public interest and debate. The installation aligns with Banksy’s recent provocative works and remains standing as authorities have no plans for removal, highlighting the ongoing tension between ephemeral street art and public preservation.
keyPoints: The statue appeared overnight in Waterloo Place, London, showing a suited man blinded by a wind-blown flag.
Banksy’s signature was found on the statue’s base, and his Instagram account posted a video confirming involvement.
The statue is similar in size and finish to nearby historic monuments like those of King Edward VII and Florence Nightingale.
London authorities installed safety barriers but have stated they do not intend to remove the statue as of May 1, 2026.
This work follows Banksy’s recent public art pieces, including murals on child homelessness and judicial protest imagery.
Banksy’s statues are rare compared to his murals, with notable past works including the 2004 ‘The Drinker’ sculpture.

rewrite.title: Banksy’s New London Statue Sparks Debate
rewrite.body:
A striking new statue attributed to the elusive street artist Banksy was installed overnight in central London’s Waterloo Place. The sculpture portrays a man in a suit, blinded by a flag that obscures his vision, as he unknowingly steps off a pedestal’s edge. The piece’s placement among historic monuments such as those of King Edward VII and Florence Nightingale adds layers of political and social commentary, resonating with the site’s military and leadership memorials.

Banksy’s involvement was quickly confirmed after his signature was discovered on the statue’s base and a video was posted on his Instagram showing the installation alongside iconic London imagery. The statue, likely made of fiberglass, matches the scale and finish of surrounding statues, blending into the traditional setting while delivering a provocative message about blindness to nationalism or authority.

Authorities have responded by erecting safety barriers due to growing crowds but have indicated no plans to remove the artwork, signaling a shift toward preserving Banksy’s often transient public pieces. This installation continues Banksy’s recent streak of politically charged public art, including murals addressing child homelessness and critiques of the justice system. However, the impermanence of his works remains a concern, as previous pieces have been swiftly removed or destroyed.

Banksy’s choice of sculpture—a medium he rarely uses—echoes his 2004 satirical statue ‘The Drinker,’ reinforcing his ability to challenge traditional art forms and public spaces. The new statue invites reflection on contemporary societal issues, leveraging London’s historic landscape to amplify its impact.
